sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Hannah, presented with a 3-day history of a hacking cough that started after being around her sister who had similar symptoms.
The cough began gradually and has been accompanied by coughing up green phlegm.
She also reports a sore throat, nasal congestion, and no fever, shortness of breath, body aches, nausea, or vomiting.
Her cough has not responded to over-the-counter medication.
She has a history of seasonal allergies, particularly around spring time.
Past medical history: The patient has type 2 diabetes, which has been well-managed with metformin 500mg daily.
She has not experienced any issues with the medication.
Her blood sugars have been in the range of 120.
She has been taking hydrochlorothiazide as prescribed, but has not experienced any issues with diarrhea.
Review of systems: The patient denies fever, shortness of breath, body aches, nausea, or vomiting.
She reports a sore throat and nasal congestion.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg daily for type 2 diabetes
Hydrochlorothiazide daily for hypertension
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure was elevated at 144/72, but otherwise the patient's vitals were good.
Physical exam: The patient's sinuses were tender on both sides, and there was erythema of the pharynx and symmetrical tonsils without exudates.
The patient had anterior cervical adenopathy.
The lungs were clear, and there was no wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.
The lower extremities did not have edema.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an upper respiratory infection, likely viral in nature, causing her symptoms.
Problem list: Upper respiratory infection; Type 2 diabetes; Hypertension.
Plan:
I prescribed Coricidin HBP to help with the cough.
I advised the patient to continue drinking lots of fluids, get plenty of rest, and try to manage her symptoms.
I also checked her for COVID-19 and will provide the results.
I increased the patient's hydrochlorothiazide to 25mg once daily to manage her hypertension.
I recommended that she continue checking her blood pressure at home a few times a week.
I will schedule a follow-up appointment for her in three months to check her hemoglobin A1c.
Follow-up: The patient will be scheduled for a follow-up appointment in three months to check her hemoglobin A1c and monitor her blood pressure and diabetes management.
